medications.drug_name: no acute pharmacological cure for the cognitive crash
medications.indication_rationale: No medication reverses an acute post-viral cognitive crash; management is cognitive/sensory rest, pacing and time, after TIA is excluded.

medications.drug_name: aspirin (if TIA strongly suspected while arranging urgent assessment)
medications.indication_rationale: If TIA is genuinely suspected (first/atypical or FAST-positive), aspirin 300mg may be appropriate per stroke pathways while arranging emergency assessment (no contraindication, NKDA) — but the priority is urgent assessment, not remote management.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 300mg if TIA suspected and no contraindication

medications.indication_rationale: No evidence for treating an acute cognitive crash; may be discussed later as low-evidence options, not acute therapy.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Cognitive-fatigue coping and anxiety support once TIA is excluded.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Cognitive and sensory rest (dark, quiet, no screens; eye mask/earplugs)
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Reducing cognitive load is the mainstay of managing a crash once serious causes are excluded.
Pragmatic pacing to stay within the cognitive energy envelope.
No evidence for acute crash management; not recommended.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Spouse to repeat a FAST if anything changes and to watch for facial droop, arm weakness, progressive/persistent symptoms, or loss of awareness.
Strict cognitive/sensory rest tonight — no screens, reading or complex conversation — in a dark, quiet room.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces cognitive load and supports recovery once serious causes are excluded.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid operating appliances/driving until recovered and assessed; have the spouse manage complex tasks tonight.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cognitive impairment creates a home-safety risk.

patient_education_points.point: Sudden trouble with words can be a sign of a mini-stroke (TIA), so we take it seriously — that's why you need to be checked in person urgently, even though this may well be your post-viral cognitive crash.
If this is the very first time or feels different from your usual crashes, or if your face droops, an arm goes weak, or your speech slurs — call 000 immediately.
Once serious causes are excluded, the treatment for these crashes is cognitive and sensory rest and pacing — there's no pill that fixes it, and supplements aren't a proven cure.
For tonight, rest in the dark and quiet, avoid screens, and don't use the stove or drive until you've recovered and been assessed.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment to exclude TIA; GP/telehealth follow-up thereafter for the cognitive phenotype
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Neurological status/TIA work-up, cognitive assessment once cleared, pacing plan, home-safety
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: FAST-positive, first/atypical or progressive symptoms, or loss of awareness — call 000.

must_include_items: Take sudden aphasia seriously; perform a spouse-assisted FAST and clarify first/atypical vs stereotyped recurrent
Arrange urgent in-person assessment to exclude TIA (000/ED if first/atypical, progressive, or FAST-positive)
Do NOT dismiss the language symptom on the Long COVID label
Provide cognitive/sensory rest, pacing and home-safety advice once serious causes are addressed
acceptable_alternatives: Aspirin 300mg if TIA strongly suspected while arranging emergency assessment
Direct 000 if any red-flag feature is present
errors_of_commission: Attributing new aphasia to Long COVID without assessment
Reflexive ambulance for a clearly stereotyped, FAST-negative recurrent crash without considering urgent in-person review
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) taking aphasia seriously with a FAST and first/atypical-vs-recurrent clarification, (2) an urgent in-person TIA-exclusion pathway (000 for first/atypical/FAST-positive), and (3) no dismissal on the Long COVID label. Calling it 'just brain fog' with no assessment is a fail.
